old_claim.note: Expert systems encode knowledge bases and inference rules in software; software engineering is required to build dependable systems.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from software engineering to Symbolic AI & Expert Systems. Early systems such as DENDRAL are scoped here as logic- and knowledge-representation techniques that predate modern software engineering practice and do not require the broad software engineering discipline as a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: DENDRAL history account discusses a 1960s medical reasoning system that uses declarative rules and symbol manipulation before software engineering was a named discipline in the modern sense.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The MIT source frames expert systems as an AI methodology grounded in symbolic knowledge representation, with implementation details constrained by early AI project context.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removing the direct software-engineering dependency because the source shows that the conceptual primitive for this node predates and is only indirectly connected to later software-engineering process doctrines.

ontology_before: Symbolic AI & Expert Systems depended directly on a broad software engineering prerequisite.
ontology_after: The node keeps AI-scoped prerequisites only, while software engineering remains implicit context for modern deployment.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: software_engineering is no longer a direct predecessor. Preserved: conceptual dependence on Artificial Intelligence (Early) and Formal Logic (Syllogism).
would_reject_if: A clear primary-source timeline showed DENDRAL-like expert systems depended on a defined software-engineering stack in practice before its claimed scope date.
The node scope is narrowed to modern commercial expert-system products that require full software lifecycle engineering as prerequisite.
short_reason: This dependency was contextual modernization pressure, not a historical prerequisite.
